title: 👑 Elite challenge ⭐⭐⭐⭐: master explorer of London

questions.prompt: Complete: "Skander says, 'The Tube is faster, so I ___ take it instead of the bus.'"
questions.explanation: The speaker is "I", so the plan is "am going to" + base verb: I am going to take. "Is going to" is for he/she/it, "going to" drops the verb be, and "am go to" removes the -ing of going.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Of all the streets here, Oxford Street is ___, so let's start there."
questions.explanation: "Busy" ends in -y, so the superlative changes y → i and adds -est, with "the": the busiest ✓. "The busyest" keeps the y wrongly, "busiest" forgets "the", and "the most busy" is wrong for a short -y adjective. The trap is not applying the y → i spelling.

questions.prompt: One sentence has a mistake. Which one? (1) London is bigger than Tunis. (2) The Shard is the tallest building. (3) This bus is more slower than the Tube. (4) We are going to visit the park.
questions.explanation: Sentence 3 marks the comparative twice: "more slower" ✓ is the error — it should be "slower than" (or "more slow" is also wrong; slow is short, so "slower"). Sentences 1, 2 and 4 correctly use "bigger than", "the tallest" and "are going to". The common trap is adding "more" to an adjective that already has -er.

questions.prompt: Turn this into a suggestion with -ing: "Let's visit the museum." → "___ the museum?"
questions.explanation: After "How about", the verb takes -ing: How about visiting the museum? ✓ "How about visit / to visit / visits" all use the wrong verb form. The common trap is keeping the base verb the way you would after "Let's".

questions.prompt: Complete: "The traffic today is ___ than yesterday, so we are going to walk."
questions.explanation: "Bad" is irregular: its comparative is "worse" (than) ✓. "Worst" is the superlative (the top of a group), while "badder" and "more bad" do not exist. The common trap is using the superlative "worst" where a comparison of two days needs "worse".

questions.prompt: Complete: "For visitors, the British Museum is ___ museum in this part of London."
questions.explanation: "Famous" is a longer adjective, so the superlative is "the most famous" ✓. "The famousest" does not exist, "most famous" forgets "the", and "the more famous" is a comparative, not a superlative. The trap is dropping "the" or choosing the comparative.
